coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots as corrosion inhibitor for ultra-high voltage etched Al foil
coredata.dc:creator: Bao, Hongliang
Lian, Yue
Qiao, Zhengshan
Zhang, Shuan
Meng, Xianfu
Ma, Kunsong
Zhu, Deqiu
Zhao, Jing
Zhang, Huaihao
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method, and introduced into the preparation process of ultra-high voltage etched Al foil as corrosion inhibitor, in order to alleviate its over-etching (tunnels clustering and overall thinning), thus to increase its specific capacitance. N-CQDs were characterized by ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). The effects of N-CQDs on etched tunnels, cross section of tunnel tunnels and electrochemical behavior of etched Al foil were investigated by scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), open circuit potential (OCP), cyclic voltammetry curve (CV), potentiodynamic polarization curves (PDP) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As a result, when 100 mg·L−1 N-CQDs is added, the over-etching on etched Al foil surface is obviously modified, together with the extended depth, the relieved tunnels clustering, and the lowed Al foil weight loss ratio. Meanwhile, compared with the sample without N-CQDs, the specific capacitance of the ultra-high voltage formed foils prepared by anodizing at 600 V increased by 30 % from 0.49 μF·cm−2 to 0.64 μF·cm−2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
N-doped
Etched Al foil
Specific capacitance
Over-etching

Xinjiang Production and Construction Corps financial science and technology plan project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Xinjiang Production and Construction Corps financial science and technology plan project ( 2021AA001 ), Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (grant number KYCX21_3198 ), and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD). The related measure and analysis instrument for this work was supported by the Testing Center of Yangzhou University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Corrosion inhibition model of N-CQDs for etched foils.
Synthesis diagram (a), UV–visible absorption spectrum (b) and FITR (c) of N-CQDs.
HRTEM image (a) and size distribution histogram (b) for N-CQDs.
XPS spectrum of etched foil surface of N-CQDs-0 and N-CQDs-100 (a); C 1s (b); N 1s (c) (d); O 1s (e) (f); Al 2p (g) (h).
SEM images of samples (N-CQDs-0 (a) and N-CQDs-100 (b)); Al, C, N and O elements distribution of samples; EDS spectrum of samples (c) and (d).
XRD spectra of samples.
SEM images of etched foil N-CQDs-0 (a, c) and N-CQDs-100 (b, d).
Cross section topography of etched foil N-CQDs-0 (a, b) and N-CQDs-100 (c, d).
Pit size and tunnel length distribution of samples.
SEM of ultra-high voltage anodized foil samples: N-CQDs-0 (a, b); N-CQDs-100 (c, d); SEM of the Al2O3 dielectric film (e).
Open circuit curve of samples.
Cyclic voltammetry curve of etched foil samples at the scanning speed 10 mV·s−1.
Tafel curves of s etched foil samples at the scanning speed 10 mV·s−1.
Bode diagram (a, b), Nyquist diagram (c) and fitted circuit diagram (d) of samples.
Relative element content of samples.
Relevant parameters of samples.
Fitting data of cyclic voltammetry of etched foil samples.
Fitting parameters of Tafel curve of etched foil samples.
AC impedance fitting parameters of samples.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots as corrosion inhibitor for ultra-high voltage etched Al foil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: XinJiang West HongYuan Electronics Co., Ltd, Xinjiang 832061, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: XinJiang West HongYuan Electronics Co., Ltd, Xinjiang 832061, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: YangZhou HongYuan Electronics. Co., Ltd, Yangzhou 225002, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: YangZhou HongYuan Electronics. Co., Ltd, Yangzhou 225002, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng, 224051, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method, and introduced into the preparation process of ultra-high voltage etched Al foil as corrosion inhibitor, in order to alleviate its over-etching (tunnels clustering and overall thinning), thus to increase its specific capacitance. N-CQDs were characterized by ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). The effects of N-CQDs on etched tunnels, cross section of tunnel tunnels and electrochemical behavior of etched Al foil were investigated by scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), open circuit potential (OCP), cyclic voltammetry curve (CV), potentiodynamic polarization curves (PDP) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As a result, when 100 mg·L−1 N-CQDs is added, the over-etching on etched Al foil surface is obviously modified, together with the extended depth, the relieved tunnels clustering, and the lowed Al foil weight loss ratio. Meanwhile, compared with the sample without N-CQDs, the specific capacitance of the ultra-high voltage formed foils prepared by anodizing at 600 V increased by 30 % from 0.49 μF·cm−2 to 0.64 μF·cm−2.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, with the rapid development of some fields, such as new energy vehicles, high-speed rail and clean energy power generation (photovoltaic, wind power), the demand for ultra-high voltage Al electrolytic capacitors, used for rectification and filtering with high voltage resistance, large specific capacitance and low leakage current, has begun to rise [1–4]. As the core material of ultra-high voltage Al electrolytic capacitor, its anode foil is key to the performance of capacitor products [5]. At present, the preparation process of ultra-high voltage anode foil is as follows: the aluminum foil was first etched by direct current (DC) in HCl-H2SO4 system, then enlarged in HNO3 system to gain ultra-high voltage etched foil, which is further anodized by high voltage to achieve the anode foil [6–9]. Generally, during this preparation (Fig. 1(a)), over-etching is easy to occur on etched foil, resulting in the tunnel clusters, the insufficient depth of etched tunnel and the thinning of the etched foil, which reduces the specific capacitance and mechanical properties of anode foils [10,11].

As previously reported, the addition of corrosion inhibitor in the process of electrochemical corrosion of aluminum foil can effectively reduce the over-etching phenomenon, ensuring that the corrosion happens in the tunnel as much as possible [12]. In this way, a high specific capacitance can be achieved, as well as it good mechanical properties. Recently, the reported types of corrosion inhibitors mainly include inorganic corrosion inhibitors (such as phosphoric acid and sodium hexametaphosphate, which are mainly phosphoric acid and its salt compounds) and organic corrosion inhibitors [13] (such as sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS), polystyrene sulfonic acid (PSSA), polyethylene glycol, etc.) [14–16]. The dominant mechanism of above corrosion inhibitor is: the corrosion inhibitor forms an adsorption film on etched foil surface to lower the contact between Al atoms and corrosion liquid, which slows down the over-etching phenomenon. Meanwhile, due to the large size commonly, the corrosion inhibitor molecules are difficult to enter into the tunnels. Thus the tunnel grows along the vertical direction, which will effectively augment the tunnels depth, the specific surface area and the specific capacitance [16]. However, there are also a series of problems in the application of corrosion inhibitors: (1) The adsorption of inorganic corrosion inhibitor is usually poor, so its effect on the mechanical properties and specific capacitance of corrosion foil is limited, which make it generally not be used alone; and (2) Although the adsorption of organic corrosion inhibitors on foil surface is better in common due to the effect of their surface functional groups, some of them are toxic and harmful to the environment, such as organic compounds containing aromatic and heterocyclic rings [17].

N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), a new zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, present the characteristics of easy availability of raw materials, wide range of sources and simple synthesis process, as well as low toxicity, biocompatibility and degradability. At present, they have been studied as a corrosion inhibitor in the corrosion protection of carbon steel, while less researched in the preparation of anode foils for aluminum electrolytic capacitors [18,19].

In this paper, the N-CQDs, introduced into the preparation process of ultra-high pressure etched foil, will form a protective film on etched foil surface through physisorption and chemisorption, so as to reduce the contact between Al and NO3 −, and achieve the purpose of lowering the over-etching of Al matrix. From Fig. 1(a), there are three main action ways to form the protective film of N-CQDs for physisorption [18–21]. Firstly, NO3 − ions are adsorbed on etched foil/solution interface under corrosion potential through electrostatic attraction due to the positive charges on interface. Meanwhile, N-CQDs tend to protonate in acidic solution (N-CQDs + H+ → [N-CQDsH+]). In this case, the protonated N-CQDs was electrostatically adsorbed at the NO3 − site on etched foil surface to form an adsorbed film (Fig. 1(a)). Secondly, the metal Al on etched foil surface is easily oxidized into Al2O3. The adsorbed films can also be produced by hydrogen bond formation between N-CQDs and oxide film Al2O3, which is more prevalent for protonated N atoms because the positive charges on N atom facilitate hydrogen bond formation. As shown in Fig. 1(a), in addition to physisorption, the chemisorption also exists. As for the unprotonated N-CQDs, the lone pair electrons outside N atom can combine with the low energy vacancy electron orbitals in metal Al to form a coordination link, thus directly adsorbing unprotonated N-CQDs on etched foil surface to form an adsorption film [22]. In general, the N-CQDs protective film, formed by above physisorption and chemisorption function, can effectively reduce the over-etching during the preparation of etched foil, increase the depth of etched tunnels (Fig. 1(b) and (c)), and lower the occurrence of merged tunnels (Fig. 1(d) and (e)) and the overall thinning of foil. Thus, the specific surface area and specific capacitance of etched foil can be improved.

The ultra-high voltage Al foil specimen fully annealed (Showa Denko Co., Ltd., Japan) was 127 μm thickness, 99.99 wt% purity, and 〈100〉 cubic texture fraction makes up >95 %. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. The urea (CH4N2O), citric acid (CA), organic silicone, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), nitric acid (HNO3), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), anhydrous ethanol (C2H6O), boric acid (BA), ammonium pentaborane (NH4B5O8) and iodine (I2) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China.

1.5 g CH4N2O and 1.5 g CA were dissolved in 8 mL deionized water, then placed in an ultrasonic dispersion instrument for 30 min. After that, the produced transparent solution was transferred to the polytetrafluoroethylene inner tank to be heated at 170 °C for 4 h, thus to acquire the brown solution by cooling. Finally, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm to separate the large particles. The centrifuged solution was further filtered by 0.22 μm filter paper, and the N-CQDs corrosion inhibitor was achieved after drying.

The Al foil was cut to a size of 1.0 cm × 1.5 cm, coated with 704 silicone rubber and etched on single side. Firstly, the prepared Al foil was soaked in 4 wt% NaOH at 90 °C for 90 s, in order to remove the uneven oxidation layer and oil on its surface. Then the above-mentioned foil was immersed in a mixed solution of 3 wt% H2SO4 + 15 wt% HNO3 at 60 °C for 150 s, which makes the oxidation layer uniform on etched foil surface. Subsequently, the Al foil was DC electrochemical etched under current density 0.4 A·cm−2 in a solution of 40 wt% H2SO4 + 5 wt% HCl and keeping 75 s at 70 °C. Finally, the etched foil as anode with platinum electrode as cathode was immersed in 7 wt% HNO3 + 0.4 wt% Al3+ solution for tunnel expansion holding 150 s at 70 °C and 0.1 A·cm−2. The effects of N-CQDs concentration on over-etching of Al foil, such as the morphology of surface and cross section (etched tunnels images, distribution and density) and electrochemical behavior, were studied in detail.

The N-CQDs was analyzed and characterized by ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis, LAMBDA 650, USA), Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR, TENSOR27, Germany) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN, USA).

The chemical composition of the adsorbed film formed by N-CQDs inhibitor on the etching foil surface was analyzed by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, USA). The surface and cross section morphology of etched foil were available by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Carl Zeiss AG S-4800, Germany). To obtain the surface information of as-prepared etched aluminum, the samples were monitored by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, λ = 1.5406 Å, tube voltage = 40 kV, tube current = 40 mA, using CuKα radiation).

Preparation of anodized Al film replica: the etched foil as anode was placed in 5 wt% H3BO3 at 90 °C and 110 V for anodization treatment, so as to from the anodic oxide film inside etched foil. Then the unetched side of etched foil was sanded with fine sandpaper to expose Al matrix. Afterwards, the etched foil was immersed in CH3OH containing 10 wt% I2 at room temperature for 10 h–12 h to remove Al matrix. Finally, the cross-section morphology of oxide film replica sample was observed by SEM.

The corrosion inhibition performance of N-CQDs on etched foils was investigated by an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Shanghai, China). The prepared corrosion foil was used as anode, with platinum electrode as the opposite electrode and Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode. Then the open circuit potential (OCP), cyclic voltammetry curve (CV), potentiodynamic polarization curves (PDP) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of etch foil were tested in 7 wt% HNO3 + 0.4 wt% Al3+ electrolyte at different N-CQDs concentrations (0 mg·L−1, 25 mg·L−1, 50 mg·L−1, 100 mg·L−1 and 200 mg·L−1) at 70 °C. Based on N-CQDs adding concentration, these etched Al foil samples were named N-CQDs-0, N-CQDs-25, N-CQDs-50, N-CQDs-100 and N-CQDs-200, respectively. Finally, the specific capacitance of foil was evaluated by LCR digital bridge (the electrolyte 10 wt% NH4B5O8 with the test frequency 100 Hz, as shown in Fig. S1).

Fig. 2(a) and (b) shows the synthesis diagram and UV–visible absorption spectrum of N-CQDs, respectively. Obviously, N-CQDs present the absorption peaks at 248 nm and 318 nm, owing to the n-π* electron transition of CO and NO [23]. From the inset in Fig. 2(b), the color of N-CQDs under visible light (left) and ultraviolet light (right) display deep-blue fluorescence under ultraviolet irradiation, indicating a fluorescent property of N-CQDs [22]. Based the above results, the N-CQDs was successfully synthesized by hydrothermal reaction.

The FTIR spectrums of N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 2(c). Regarding the N-CQDs, their strong and wide absorption peaks at 3500 cm−1 are indexed to the stretching vibration of NH and OH alcohol hydroxyl groups [24]. The peak at 1357 cm−1 signifies the in-plane bending vibration of CO, whereas the peaks at 1635 cm−1 and 2065 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of CN and NCO, respectively [25,26]. In conclusion, the surface of N-CQDs prepared by one step hydrothermal process possessed abundant functional groups with a network polymer structure.

High resolution TEM images and size distribution of N-CQDs are presented in Fig. 3 . Well-dispersed N-CQDs nanoparticles with average sizes around 11.5 nm were shown in Fig. 3(a) and (b). High resolution TEM (Fig. 3(b)) clearly revealed that the synthetic N-CQDs offer noticeable lattice fringes with interplanar distance of around 0.32 nm, showing a high-crystalline structure and in accordance with the reported values of the (002) lattice plane of graphite [21,27].

In order to analyze the chemical composition of the adsorbed film formed by N-CQDs inhibitor on the etching foil surface, XPS study was carried out. The samples N-CQDs-0 and N-CQDs-100 were soaked in etching solution for 30 min, respectively. In the full spectrum of XPS scanning (Fig. 4(a)), a N1s peak was detected at 400 eV on N-CQDs-100 surface, while do not appear in the blank sample N-CQDs-0 [21,25]. Meanwhile, the simultaneous analysis of specific elements reveals that the N element of N-CQDs-0 is 2.42 %, which is from HNO3 solution in etching process, leading to NO3 − adsorption on etching foil surface. In contrast, as for N-CQDs-100 sample, its N content significantly increases to 6.01 %. Combined with the previous FTIR and UV–vis analyses, a higher concentration of N element existed in N-CQDs-100. Consequently, it can be inferred that the presence of the N1s peak on N-CQDs-100 originates from N-CQDs, thereby verifying their adsorption onto foils surface and successful N doping [28,29].

Split-peak fitting was performed for C1s, N1s, O1s and Al2p peaks under different conditions (Fig. 4(b–h)). For N-CQDs-0, its N element in N1s spectrum from NO3 − in etching solution exhibits a single peak at 399.38 eV (Fig. 4(c)), along with the O1s alone peak at 532.28 eV (Fig. 4(e)), corresponding to O2 − (aluminum oxide, AlOOH or Al2O3) [30]. Additionally, its Al2p spectrum displays two peaks at 72.6 eV and 74.8 eV (Fig. 4(g)), which respectively match with the surface metals Al and Al3+ (aluminum oxide, indicated by the O1s spectrum) [31,32]. Comparatively, as for the sample N-CQDs-100, its C1s spectrum exhibits three distinct peaks at 248.8 eV (CC), 285.8 eV (CO), and 287.9 eV (NCO) (Fig. 4(b)). These chemical bonds were all attributed to N-CQDs, which also indicates the presence of N-CQDs on etching foil surface as an adsorption layer [33]. Furthermore, three peaks in the N1s spectrum represent CN (400.18 eV), CN (401.8 eV) and NAl (402.7 eV) (Fig. 4(d)), and the presence of NAl indicates the coordination bond between N-CQDs and the etching foil [34,35]. Additionally, the O 1s XPS spectrum of N-CQDs-100 displayed two new peaks at 531.2 eV (CO) and 533.7 eV (CO) (Fig. 4 (e)), as observed in the C1s spectrum results as well. The above formation of carbon‑oxygen bonds further confirms the existence of an adsorption film consisting of N-CQDs on etching foil surface [23]. From the Al2p spectrum, evidently, the addition of N-CQDs results in the presence of AlON (79.03 eV) (Fig. 4(f)), attributed to the bonding between nitrogen and surface oxide on etching foil through oxygen atoms [36] [37]. Meanwhile, the attenuation of the Al3+ peak with Al peak disappearance can be ascribed to the formation of N-CQDs adsorption film on etching foil (Fig. 4(f)). As we all know, XPS detection depth typically does not exceed 10 nm, it can be inferred that the thickness of this adsorbed film may be larger than 10 nm.

Fig. 5 shows the morphology and element distribution of etched foils in different solutions. Compared with the sample N-CQDs-0 (Fig. 5(a)), the surface of the N-CQDs-100 was uniformly covered with a film (Fig. 5(b)), resulting in less exposed Al matrix and reduced over corrosion. Meanwhile, from the element distribution diagram, except for Al with similar distribution and amounts (Fig. 5(a1) and (b1)), the C, N and O distribution and amounts (Fig. 5(a2–a4) and Fig. 5(b2–b4)) of etched sample N-CQDs-100 were more evenly and larger than that of N-CQDs-0 significantly. The quantitative results of EDS analysis have been provided in Fig. 5(c, d) and Table 1 . Obviously, 55.39 % Al element in N-CQDs-100 sample is shown less than the N-CQDs-0, which comes from large amount of Al substrate on sample surface of N-CQDs-100. Meanwhile, 24.97 % of the C element and 6.95 % of the N element were also discovered greater than the N-CQDs-0, which was attributed to N-CQDs adsorption film of sample surface. Additionally, the O element content of sample N-CQDs-100, from Al2O3 on etched sample surface and N-CQDs, is also higher than that of N-CQDs-0, just from Al2O3 on etched sample surface. Clearly, the N-CQDs inhibitor in solution was well adsorbed on etched foil, which was conducive to reducing the contact between the corrosive ion and the metal Al in subsequent process, so as to improve a favorable inhibition performance for the etched foil.

From the XRD pattern of the etched foil surface phase in different solutions in Fig. 6 , both samples reveal the strong characteristic peak at 44.8°, which matches well with the diffraction crystal plane of Al (200) (JCPDS card No. 04-0708) from the aluminum matrix in samples. The diffraction crystal planes of BOE (γ-AlOOH, also known as boehmite) (051) (JCPDS card No. 21-1307) and Al2O3 (011), (115), and (111) (JCPDS card No. 10-0414) were observed at 47.2°, 17.8°, 28.4°, and 40.3°, respectively [38].

With the increase of corrosion stage, the etched tunnel diameter gradually augments, and its shape changes from “cuboidal” to “circular” (Fig. S2.). From the ultra-high voltage etched foils surface morphology in Fig. 7(a–d), a large number of cylindrical pits are produced on etched foil. Compared with the sample N-CQDs-0 (Fig. 7(a) and (c)), the N-CQDs-100 has fewer pits-merging (the white areas comparison in Fig. 7(a) and (b)), also with the higher density (the red areas comparison in Fig. 7(c) and (d)) and more uniform distribution of surface etch pits (Fig. 7(b) and (d)). As for the cross section topography (Fig. 8 ), the tunnels are distributed in a “cylindrical” shape in depth, along with the generated columnar branch tunnels at its tail. Obviously, for N-CQDs-100 (Fig. 8(c) and (d)), the tunnels continuously developed along vertical direction, showing its significantly increased depth.

Fig. 9(a–e) and (f–j) respectively show the pit size and tunnel length distribution of etched foils obtained by ImageJ topography analysis. From Fig. 9(a–e), with the addition of N-CQDs, the proportion of large size (>2.8 μm) pits gradually decreases, which is conducive to reducing pit clusters on etched foil surface because large size etched pits usually have a higher probability of pit clusters [38]. With the increase of N-CQDs concentration, the etched pit proportion (1.6 μm–1.8 μm) gradually boosted with the lowered curve dispersion. When N-CQDs concentration reach 100 mg·L−1 (Fig. 9(d)), the pit size in the range of 1.6 μm–1.8 μm reaches the maximum value of 0.42, along with the minimum curve dispersion. From the above results, the addition of N-CQDs in corrosion process can make the large size etched pits amounts and the pit clusters probability decline, achieving more concentrated etched pits size distribution, so as to lower the over-etching on etched foil surface. In terms of the cross-section morphology of etched foils (Fig. 9(f–j)), with the increase of N-CQDs concentration, the curve dispersion enlarges, and its peak value steadily moves in the direction of larger value. Meanwhile, the etched tunnels amounts (>14 μm) is gradually increased up to the maximum value of N-CQDs-100 (Fig. 9(j)), which makes the longitudinal space of etched foil enlarged effectively.

The relevant parameters of Al tunnels in Table 2 are acquired according to morphology analysis by ImageJ. With the rise of N-CQDs concentration, the pit density enlarges from 0.854 × 107 ea∙cm−2 to 1.105 × 107 ea∙cm−2, and the average pit size lowers from 2.16 μm to 1.77 μm. At the same time, the tunnels depth of etched foil and the specific capacitance of anodized foil also boosted gradually, showing their maximum value 18.10 μm and 0.64 μF∙cm−2 at sample N-CQDs-0. Therefore, the N-CQDs can lessen the ineffective and over-etching of etched foils by corrosive anions. Thus, the specific surface area of etched foil and the specific capacitance of the anodized foil are enhanced. Comparatively, the tunnels length and the specific capacitance decline to 16.20 μm and 0.58 μF∙cm−2 at sample N-CQDs-200, respectively. Clearly, the excessive amount of N-CQDs inhibitor leads to the amounts rise of small size etched pits (<1.4 μm), which are easy to become the “dead tunnels” with short longitudinal growth, so as to lower the specific surface and specific capacitance [8]. Finally, the weight loss rate of corroded foil gradually decreases with the rise of N-CQDs concentration as shown in Table 2. Generally, the N-CQDs can be adsorbed on the foil surface to reduce the contact between Al metal and the aggressive anion, resulting in less Al matrix corrosion with a lower weight loss rate.

Fig. 10(a–d) affords the surface morphology of the ultra-high voltage anodized foils. Their surface are uniformly covered with an Al2O3 dielectric film (Fig. 10(b) and (d)). Meanwhile, compared with the sample N-CQDs-0, the anodized foil N-CQDs-100 demonstrates the smoother surface, the smaller height difference and the fewer merging tunnels. Obviously, the corrosion inhibition of N-CQDs reduce over-etching and tunnel clusters occurrence, and the thinning of etched foils. From the morphology of Al2O3 dielectric film in Fig. 10(e), after 600 V high voltage anodizing, a layer of Al2O3 film with large thickness and dense structure is generated along the tunnel, with the thickness of the Al2O3 dielectric film reaching 286.5 nm.

The open circuit potential (OCP) curve of the etched Al foils in the different solutions at ambient temperature is presented in Fig. 11 . With the corrosion processing for Al foil sample, its initial E ocp was −0.6317 V, and moved towards a negative potential with the extension of etching time in the absence of N-CQDs. This changes were attributed to that the Al metal on the etched foil surface is easily oxidized into Al2O3 film in air. Meanwhile, when the corrosion reaction begins, more active metal Al is exposed accompanying by Al2O3 film shedding, which generates the gradually increasing tendency of corrosion reaction [39,40]. The initial E ocp of N-CQDs-100 is −0.5138 V, larger than that of sample N-CQDs-0, which can be attributed to the formation of a N-CQDs adsorption protective layer on etched Al foil surface [41].

The cyclic voltammetry curves of etched foils are shown in Fig. 12 at the scanning speed is 10 mV·s−1 in the potential range −0.9 V–0 V. According to the OCP curve data, the etched aluminum foil was immersed in the solution for 10 min before testing, in order to reach a stable state of the open circuit potential in the system. After the test started, the anodic current of etched foil first undergone a small leakage current and then began to boost rapidly at −0.77 V, which indicates the beginning of expanded tunnels in etched foil. From relevant data of cyclic voltammetry curve in Table 3 , the anode current density decreased with the enhancement of N-CQDs concentration, and the slope of curve also gradually are reduced to the minimum value of 0.097 for N-CQDs-200. In summary, due to the abundant functional groups on N-CQDs surface, the N-CQDs molecules can be adsorbed on etched foil to form a protection film, so as to plays a good corrosion inhibition effect [23,38].

From the potentiodynamic polarization curve in Fig. 13 , the trend of cathodic and anodic polarization curves of etched foil did not change with N-CQDs concentration, which revealed that N-CQDs inhibitor do not influence the reaction mechanism of cathode and anode [20]. In general, corrosion current density (i corr) is a kinetic parameter reflecting the corrosion reaction rate, and corrosion potential (E corr) as a thermodynamic parameter shows the tendency of corrosion reaction occurrence. Table 4 lists the parameters of the potentiodynamic polarization curve. By calculating the potentiodynamic polarization curves, the following parameters were obtained in Table 4, E corr, i corr and corrosion inhibition efficiency (η). η values could be calculated as following Eq. 1 [26]:
(1)
η =
i corr 0
−
i corr
i corr 0
× 100
%
where,
i corr 0
represents the current density of N-CQDs-0;
i corr
is the current density of samples in different N-CQDs concentration solutions, respectively.

Obviously, compared with the sample N-CQDs-0, i corr and E corr of samples with N-CQDs decreased and shifted positively, respectively. The results showed that N-CQDs can be adsorbed on the surface of etched foil, which lowers the reaction rate and corrosion tendency [42].

The corrosion resistance of aluminum foil was evaluated by electrochemical impedance spectra. In Fig. 14(a) and (b), the general appearance the Bode and Nyquist diagrams of etched foil were similar, exhibiting that N-CQDs do not change the reaction mechanism of etched foil, which is consistent with the conclusion of previous potentiodynamic polarization curve. With the boost of N-CQDs concentration, the impedance modulus |Z| in the low frequency region increases (Fig. 14(a)), which indicates that N-CQDs was adsorbed on etched foil to hinder electron migration, foster the bulk reactance and reduce the corrosion rate [24]. In Fig. 14(b), the phase angle of the Bode diagram shows that only one charge-transfer relaxation time constant exists in the reaction process, and the peak height enlarges with the increase of N-CQDs concentration, which evidences the strong adsorption response of N-CQDs at the interface between etched foil and solution [29,43]. The Nyquist diagram in Fig. 14(c) clearly resembles an incomplete arc of capacitance-reactance, which reveals N-CQDs controlling corrosion on etched foil mainly through a charge transfer mechanism. Meanwhile, The arc radius increases with the rise of the N-CQDs concentration, resulting in more adsorbed N-CQDs, higher impedance and lower corrosive effect on etched foil, while without changing the reaction mechanism [44,45].

The fitted equivalent circuit diagram is shown in Fig. 14(d). In order to explain the role of N-CQDs in the preparation of corrosion foil in detail, the ZView software was used to fit the AC impedance spectrum. As for the equivalent circuits, R s, R p and C dl are the solution resistance, polarization resistance and the constant phase element, respectively. At the same time, the corrosion inhibition efficiency (η) of the etched foil in different solution was calculated by the polarization resistance R p, and the calculation formula is shown as Eq. (2) [46]:
(2)
η =
R p
−
R p 0
R p
× 100
%
where,
R p 0
represents the polarization resistance of N-CQDs-0;
R p
is the polarization resistance of samples in various N-CQDs concentration solutions, respectively.

From fitting parameters of AC impedance spectrum in Table 5 , with the N-CQDs concentrations rising, the solution resistance R s changes little. Obviously, N-CQDs do not change the property of etching solution. In Table 5, the polarization resistance R p increases from 1.42 Ω∙cm−2 to 5.28 Ω∙cm−2, which reveals that the N-CQDs inhibitor on etched foil can effectively retard the electron migration rate on foil surface. Meanwhile, due to N-CQDs function, the specific surface area of Al foil declines, resulting in continuous C dl reduction of etched foil. Furthermore, the corrosion inhibition efficiency (η) enhances with the increase of N-CQDs. Clearly, under the 200 mg·L−1 CQDs, the maximum corrosion inhibition efficiency on the etched foil N-CQDs-200 reached 73.1 %, indicating that the addition of CQDs in solution can effectively inhibit the over-etching of the etched foil, which is consistent with the results of the potentiodynamic polarization curve. While, with the excessive amount of N-CQDs, its adsorption response on etched foil surface reaches the maximum, which makes the adsorption film have a significant barrier effect on NO3 −. As a result, the NO3 − amount on etched foil surface was reduced, leading to the insufficient enlarging tunnel capacity, which lowers the tunnel aperture, depth and the specific capacitance. In conclusion, according to the relevant data of the etched tunnel and the specific capacitance of anodized foils, the 100 mg∙L−1 is the optimal addition concentration of N-CQDs for ultra-high voltage etched foils preparation.

In this work, N-CQDs were successfully synthesized. Also, its effects as a corrosion inhibitor on the property of ultra-high voltage etched Al foil and the specific capacitance of anodized foil was investigated. As the result, the N-CQDs inhibitor can inhibit the over-etching (tunnels clustering and overall thinning) on etched foil, which facilitates the evenly distributed tunnels, the increased specific surface area and the extended tunnels depth, so as to boost the specific capacitance of anodized foil. The N-CQDs, with abundant NH, OH, CCO and CN functional groups by hydrothermal method, is helpful to the favorable adsorption of N-CQDs inhibitor on etched foil, so as to reduce its over-etching. Meanwhile, the N-CQDs on etched foil forms a protective film to isolate the contact between corrosive anions and surface metal Al, which lower the over-etching of Al foil, thus achieving the evenly distributed etched tunnels, the prolonged tunnels length and the reduced weight loss rate. The specific capacitance of the ultra-high voltage anodized foil was boosted by about 30 %, from 0.49 μF·cm−2 of N-CQDs-0 to 0.64 μF·cm−2 of N-CQDs-100.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Xinjiang Production and Construction Corps financial science and technology plan project (2021AA001), Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (grant number KYCX21_3198), and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD). The related measure and analysis instrument for this work was supported by the Testing Center of Yangzhou University.
